title: A Cheat Sheet to the Types of Glass Used in Stemware
summary:
If you’ve been considering upping your glassware game but don’t know where to start, learn these main glass types first.

The post A Cheat Sheet to the Types of Glass Used in Stemware appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Far from being a mere delivery system for your Merlot or Manhattan, a well-made glass enhances the drinking experience by looking (and feeling) aesthetically pleasing. In addition to coming in different shapes and styles, stemware is crafted from all sorts of materials—each with its own benefits and drawbacks. 

If you’ve been considering upping your glassware game but don’t know where to start, learn these glass types first. Below, sommeliers, bartenders, and other drinks professionals offer their takes on which to pick up—and when to use them.

The Glass Types in Wine Stemware, Explained

All glass is not created equally. Learn these common terms before you make your next purchase.

Machine-Made

The least expensive stemware is machine-made, where molten glass is poured or pressed into molds. While the resulting glassware is uniform and durable, it also tends to be thicker, with an unsightly seam and a telltale indentation on the bottom of the base. 

“While machine-made glassware may not create the ideal aesthetics for an upscale wine tasting, it ensures you get a consistent sip, especially when comparing wines,” says Jim Pennella, owner of Farang Wine and Harmony School Vineyard, in Napa.

Machine-Blown

A step up is machine-blown, which uses an automation process to mimic glassblowing techniques, rendering a lighter, more refined glass. Riedel’s glassware is machine blown, as are Wine Enthusiast’s Pirouette and Vienna glasses.

Austin Bridges, wine director of Nostrana in Portland, Oregon, loves the shape of the Gabriel-Glas StandArt glass, which is machine-blown from one piece of crystal without joints, seams, or glue. 

“It showcases any wine beautifully from Champagne to an aged Barolo, plus it’s incredibly durable.” Moreover, its seamless design makes it more prone to chipping rather than fully breaking, making it unsightly but still usable.

Mouth-Blown and Handblown

Mouth-blown or handblown stemware (the two terms are used interchangeably) lie on the more artisanal side of the glassware spectrum. These are made by blowing air into molten glass and shaping it by hand. Zalto’s glassware, which is considered to be top of the line, is mouth-blown into molds.

“This is a handmade process,” says Anna-Christina Cabrales, Tasting Director at Wine Enthusiast. Mouth-blown glasses can take almost any shape, and no two pieces are the same—each is unique.” 

Glassware that’s handmade may be adorned with intricate designs. That means it comes with a hefty price tag—a consideration if you’re running a busy wine bar, frequently entertaining at home, or are just accident-prone. 

Crystal

Stemware choices don’t stop at the production method. Though people often use the term crystal as a synonym for glass, the phrase actually refers to a specific kind of glass that contains added minerals, often lead oxide. 

This lends a sparkly appearance that enhances the clarity, strength, and durability of the glass, and enables drinkers to see the wine within more easily. Lead-free alternatives like barium, zinc, or potassium retain the same attributes but are even more durable and can withstand being cleaned in the dishwasher. 

Lead-free crystal is the gold standard for high-end wine glasses because of how thin it can be made. “Crystal’s thin walls and ultra-thin rims allow wine to flow freely over your palate to assess the flavor of the wine fully,” Pennella says. 

Borosilicate 

Borosilicate glasses, which contain silica and boron trioxide, are another dishwasher-safe option. This is a thin, durable glass—often used in lab equipment and in the original Pyrex products—that will not crack as easily when exposed to the heat of a drying cycle.

Break-Resistant

If glassware is labeled as break-resistant or shatter-resistant, it means it’s produced from a material that makes it less prone to breakage. 

Unbreakable Glasses

While a crystal glass might be instantly destroyed if it’s knocked over on the counter, a shatter-resistant one may withstand that kind of fumble.

Finally, unbreakable “glasses” aren’t actually made of glass at all, but a copolyester plastic. Those crafted from Eastman Tritan copolyester (what Wine Enthusiast uses for its Brilliance line) are strong, easily malleable, and extremely durable, as well as FDA-approved and BPA-free.

Choosing the Right Glass Type

No matter the material, beverage pros cite one sought-after characteristic that makes for a “perfect” glass: how it performs when you drink from it.

“The main thing that affects the drinking experience is the thickness of the glassware,” says Jeff Cleveland, general manager of Birch, in Milwaukee. “The thinner the lip, the nicer it feels.” 

This is where production methods come into play. “The thinner glass walls and differing shapes of mouth-blown glassware may affect how we perceive aromas,” Pennella says. 

So, out of the dizzying array of stemware on the market, how do you know which is right for you? 

Pennella believes it’s situational. He suggests machine-made glassware that meets ISO standards for comparative tastings (these vessels are made from crystal, hold around 7 ounces of liquid, are six inches tall and around 2.5 inches wide) and mouth-blown crystal (lead or lead-free) for a more elevated experience. If you’re sipping wine by the pool, beach, or windy location, go for break-resistant or borosilicate, which, he says, has the added benefit of maintaining temperature.

If you’re a clumsy butterfingers but don’t want a clunky, thick stem, Bridges touts a seamless, thin-rimmed crystal machine-blown glass. “Glasses with seams tend to snap right at the seam during polishing or if they’re set down a bit too firmly, he says.”

When sourcing glasses for his contemporary Midwestern restaurant, Cleveland considers durability as well as aesthetics, because a delicate, thin-rimmed glass is no good if its time on the shelf is fleeting thanks to rough handling or easy breakage. For these reasons, he’s partial to Riedel’s Restaurant series, which is elegant without being too fragile.

Beau du Bois, vice president of bar and spirits at Roma Norte in San Diego, thinks about what he calls “the path of the glass,” meaning where the vessel is stored during service, where it goes once the guest has finished sipping, and how it’s cleaned—a philosophy that can be adopted by the home bartender. “It’s best to consider all these factors before getting your heart set on that new Zalto tulip, no matter how sexy it is.”

Benjamin, a steakhouse in New York, recently switched to the break-resistant Vienna glass line from Wine Enthusiast for its wine and water service. This move has helped them balance performance with aesthetics. “Our day to day breakage is a third less than with our previous line of stemware, plus the bigger sized bowls look elegant and stunning on the table,” says Albert Belegu, the restaurant’s general manager. 

Oenophiles planning on conducting serious wine tastings should consider glasses that balance durability with thinness. Sometimes, one glass isn’t enough. Cabrales advises investing in at least one pair of glasses with a good proportion of crystal, plus something more durable. 

“I want to have a sophisticated delivery into the mouth and overall feeling in the hand,” she says. “At home, I have two sets of stemware: those that help me get friends who are just starting into wine, and high-end ones that impress my nerdy wine friends. It’s all about style.”

title: Is It Time to Refresh Your Stems? 3 Signs Your Glassware Needs an Upgrade
summary:
How do you know if your glassware is up for the job—and when it’s time to upgrade? These are three signs to look for.

The post Is It Time to Refresh Your Stems? 3 Signs Your Glassware Needs an Upgrade appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There’s nothing worse than going to a restaurant, excited to order wine with dinner, and receiving it in a sub-par glass. (There’s a reason stubby stems are the meme that keeps on giving.) 

But everyone’s definition of “nice glasses” is different. I, for one, can’t stand bubbly in one of those clunky diner flutes intended for bottomless mimosas. I’m not anti-flute by any means—but I am anti-clunk. In fact, I even switched wedding caterers two weeks before my wedding because the stems they presented as the “upgraded” option were so chunky they made 1980s ashtrays look elegant. 

But one woman’s chonk is another woman’s lead-free crystal. The most important thing is that glassware performs as it should, and ideally looks beautiful doing so. So how do you know if your glassware is up for the job—and when it’s time to upgrade? It turns out, there are a few signs.

Sign #1: Your stems aren’t trustworthy anymore

If any part of your glass feels unstable, it’s not “character,” it’s a liability—and nothing kills the vibe faster than a trip to the ER for stitches. “First, look for wobble or instability on a flat surface,” says Dr. Lee Kojanis, co-founder of MITTS wine sponge. “A glass that rocks, leans, or feels even slightly off-balance can signal subtle warping or micro-damage to the stem or base—often the kind you don’t notice until it fails mid-polish or, worse, mid-toast.”

There’s also the rim. While everyone loves a delicate glass, damage to the rim should be an immediate sign it’s time to put that stem out to pasture. “A tiny nick may seem harmless, but it’s both a safety issue and a quality issue,” says Kojanis. “It can worsen with the next rinse or polish, and it disrupts the smooth, clean line that makes a glass feel refined on the lip.” 

Sign #2: They look clean, but they’re not performing

If you’ve ever poured a good wine into a clean glass expecting wow but instead getting womp womp, you’re already familiar with this sign. Some glasses look obviously cloudy. For others, the culprit is invisible until you hold the bowl up to the light and notice a film, etching, or fine scratching that builds up over years of normal use.

“Detergent film and calcium deposits leave a subtle haze, muting the wine’s aromas and flavors,” says Kurt Zalto, sixth-generation glassmaker and co-founder of glassware line Josephinenhütte. “Micro-scratches from routine cleaning can scatter light and dull the wine’s brilliance. If your glass isn’t crystal clear, it’s not performing at its best.”

And the problem isn’t just aesthetic. “That microscopic wear can also create a surface that holds onto residue and interferes with the crisp visual and aromatic experience you’re chasing,” adds Kojanis.

Given that we’re all guilty of shoving glassware into storage at some point, make sure to do the sniff test. Whether that storage looks like a top shelf in your kitchen, a rack in your dining room, or a crate somewhere with holiday decorations and your high school yearbooks, weird smells can get trapped in the bowl of your glass. 

“Cabinet odor is real and can alter how your beverage tastes,” says lifestyle content creator Lindsay Stewart. “If you store your stemware in boxes in a pantry, or in the back of a cabinet, check your glass for musty, dusty, or woody funk.”

Sign #3: Your glassware doesn’t match how you drink now

This is the sneakiest sign because it’s not about what you own—it’s about what you reach for. If your “good glasses” only come out once a decade, your glassware is failing you.

“People tend to be afraid to use their good stems as they’re afraid the glasses are too nice, and should only be used for that special bottle that they never open anyway as they never find a good occasion to drink it,” says sommelier Liora Levi, co-host of PBS show Wine First and a Riedel Ambassador.

Levi also calls out the classics that tend to linger in cabinets long past their prime. “Champagne coupes and flutes, old-fashioned white wine glasses, and thick and bulky wine glasses.” And then there’s the vacation-glass trap: “Sometimes people want to recreate the vacation feeling by using regular water or milk glasses, which is a terrible idea when you’ve spent a good amount on a nice bottle.” In other words, save that memory of sipping Chianti from a tumbler at a quaint Italian trattoria where it belongs—in the memory bank. 

If budget is the barrier, you can still make smart moves without pretending everyone needs a museum-quality collection. “Prioritize buying a ‘universal’ white and ‘universal’ red wine glass,” Levi advises. And Stewart’s budget approach will help keep the prices under control: “If you need stemware for a crowd on a budget, secondhand shopping is my favorite,” she says. “You can inexpensively curate your collection over time, including incorporating different heights, shapes, or styles. The mismatched look feels intentional when you have even numbers of each style.”

The goal isn’t perfection. Rather, it’s about having stems you trust, stems that make wine taste like itself, and stems you’ll actually use—yes, even on a Tuesday—without haze, wobble, or emergency stitches.

title: What, Exactly, Is ‘Break-Resistant’ Glassware?
summary:
While not shatterproof, break-resistant glassware can help protect your stemware from butterfingered friends.

The post What, Exactly, Is ‘Break-Resistant’ Glassware? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Do wine glasses tend to slip out of your grasp as you dry them? Does boisterous hand gesturing during wine time with friends cause you to inadvertently topple over your stemware? If so, you might want to consider investing in some break-resistant glassware.

But before you get too excited at the prospect of being able to drop vessels on the table or floor sans cracking or shattering, it’s helpful to dive into what this label on a glass does—and doesn’t—mean, and what you can expect if your butterfingers strike again.

When a glass is labeled “break-resistant,” it means it’s “engineered to withstand more impact and stress than traditional glass or crystal,” says Marshall Tilden III, President of Wine Express and Wine Education at Wine Enthusiast.

Manufacturers incorporate materials like titanium, reinforced borosilicate, or magnesium into regular glass in order to make the material more durable.

They also use processes like thermal tempering, in which glass is heated to a high temperature and then rapidly cooled, and chemical tempering, which uses a molten potassium nitrate bath to harden the glass, to strengthen its structure at a molecular level.

These techniques often result in glasses that have a bit of “give,” bolstering them with shock absorption that allows them to withstand a certain amount of impact without damage.

What to Expect from Break-Resistant Glassware

Let’s be clear: break-resistant does not mean break-proof.

While the addition of minerals and other steps during the manufacturing process can increase the resilience, you’ll still want to be sure to handle it carefully—and manage expectations.

“Extreme force, like being slammed against a hard surface, drastic temperature changes (such as pouring boiling water into a frozen glass), or deep scratches that compromise the integrity can still cause them to shatter,” Tilden cautions. After all, they’re still made from glass.

With that being said, the added hardening process does make a difference. Albert Belegu, general manager of Benjamin Restaurant Group in New York, is partial to the Vienna line of break-resistant stemware, which is machine-blown in Europe from lead-free crystal.

He touts them for their elegance, height, and large bowl, providing wine with ample room to breathe—and that the stem and base are connected as one unit. (When most glasses break, it’s at the point where the stem meets the base.) Since the glass is lead-free, it also offers remarkable clarity.

In Belegu’s experience, if one of these glasses does break, it’s more apt to come apart in larger chunks that are safer to handle. He likens it to the crack you might get from an object hitting a car’s windshield versus the complete shatter that might occur if the same object hits one of the windows (which are made from tempered glass).

Jim Pennella, owner of Farang Wine and Harmony School Vineyard in Napa, believes break-resistant glassware can be the perfect choice in certain situations.

“Break-resistant glass still allows you to smell, view, and taste your wine and might be the best option for large or family-friendly gatherings,” Pennella says.

The same logic goes for high-volume restaurants that endure frequent breakage with standard wine glasses—break-resistant stemware might be worth stocking up on.

What About ‘Shatterproof’ Glass?

If break-resistant glass is less prone to chips, cracks, and breaks, shatterproof options go a step further. “These are typically made from ultra-durable, BPA-free plastics or silicone-based materials like Tritan, meaning they won’t crack or splinter at all, even upon impact,” Tilden explains.

They’re not technically “glasses,” though depending on the style, they may have a similar appearance—like these Tritan outdoor wine glasses.

Shatterproof wine glasses can often come in bright colors and fun designs, too. They’re ideal options for barbecues, pool parties, boating, camping, outdoor concerts, or large gatherings where children (who are notorious for dropping things) will be present. 

In a nutshell, these types of stemware can still give you an elegant experience, but with less of a chance that it (and your night) will end up in pieces.

As Tilden puts it, “break-resistant glass can still maintain clarity and a lightweight feel while significantly reducing the risk of breakage.”

title: This Gooey Swiss Mac & Cheese Is Alpine Perfection
summary:
Starring nutty Gruyère, zesty yellow onion, and waxy red potatoes, Älplermagronen isn't your typical mac & cheese.

The post This Gooey Swiss Mac & Cheese Is Alpine Perfection appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There are few dishes as satisfying as macaroni and cheese, but it doesn’t exclusively belong to the American South, or need to be orange. Älplermagronen, which translates to “Alpine macaroni” in Swiss German, is a prime example of Swiss Alpine cuisine. The hearty pot of pasta and potatoes enrobed in gooey Gruyère is similar to Swiss-adjacent dishes like the käsespätzle of southern Germany and the pizzoccheri of Valtellina in northern Italy. Feel free to add small cubes of bacon or speck to the crispy onions, but don’t skip the applesauce.

Älplermagronen (Swiss Alpine Macaroni & Cheese)

  • 3 tablespoons vegetable oil
  • 1 medium yellow onion, peeled, halved, and sliced very thin
  • 2 tablespoons flour
  • 3⁄4 pound waxy potatoes (such as most red potatoes), peeled, cut into 1⁄2-inch cubes
  • 8 ounces ziti (or substitute penne or cavatappi)
  • 1 cup heavy cream
  • 1⁄8 teaspoon freshly grated nutmeg
  • 8 ounces Gruyère cheese, freshly grated
  • 2 cups applesauce, for serving
  • Salt and freshly ground pepper, to taste

Directions

In your largest skillet, heat the oil over medium heat. Toss the onions in flour and add to the pan, in a single layer if possible. Cook, stirring occasionally, until browned and crisp-edged. Set aside. (You can also cook the onions in an air fryer: Toss with flour, spray with oil to coat well, and cook at 375°F for at least 10 minutes, shaking every 3 to 4 minutes, until golden and crisp.)

Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Add the potatoes, then return the water to a boil and add the ziti. Boil until both are tender, about 10 to 12 minutes. While they cook, bring cream and nutmeg to a simmer in a small saucepan.

Drain the potatoes and pasta, then return them to the pot with the cream. Stir in Gruyère by the handful until it’s melted and creamy throughout. Season with salt and pepper to taste, if needed. Transfer to a serving dish, top with onions, and serve hot, with warmed applesauce on the side. Serves 4.

Wine Pairing

Chasselas, Switzerland’s signature white wine grape, is a classic pairing with hearty Alpine dishes like fondue and raclette. Similarly, it’s the go-to wine with älplermagronen, and not just because its crispness is refreshing between cheesy bites. It usually has complementary notes of herbs and smoke, and even a hint of orchard fruit that echoes the applesauce. Note: Chasselas is called Fendant in Valais and has other regional Swiss names as well.

TRY: Cave Caloz 2023 Fendant “La Mourzière” (Valais)

title: This Plantain Old Fashioned Flew Straight in From Nairobi
summary:
With rum, chocolate bitters, and homemade plantain-banana syrup, the plantain old fashioned is ready to shake up your at-home cocktail menu.

The post This Plantain Old Fashioned Flew Straight in From Nairobi appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
At Nairobi’s Hero Bar—a whimsical space inside the Trademark Hotel, decorated with anime murals and superhero figurines—the Plantain Old Fashioned is a mainstay on the colorful, comic-book-style menu. The drink was originally created for AJABU, a cocktail conference in Cape Town, where it was part of a lineup showcasing ingredients and flavors typical of African cuisine.

Strong and stirred, the cocktail is balanced by the subtle sweetness of plantain. While they are part of the same family as the banana, plantains are starchier and need to be cooked, which yields a caramelized sweetness that shines in drinks made with aged spirits. (While this version uses rum, Hero has also made it with Cognac and whiskey.)

This recipe is simpler than what’s served at the bar. At the bar, the original starts with a fat-washed spirit, made with the butter that was used to cook the plantains; the cooked fruit is then turned into a plantain-banana syrup. This simplified version won’t have the same buttery texture, but still offers a delightful banana-bread note, accented with chocolate bitters.

Plantain Old Fashioned

Adapted from Kelvin Thairu, bars manager, and Richie Barrow, general manager, Hero Bar, Nairobi, Kenya

Ingredients

1 1⁄2 ounces aged rum
1⁄2 ounce plantain-banana oleo saccharum*
2 dashes Scrappy’s Chocolate Bitters
Fried plantain slice (or plantain chip), for garnish

Directions

Stir all ingredients with ice. Strain into a rocks glass over a large piece of ice. Garnish with a fried plantain slice or plantain chip.

 

*To Make Plantain-Banana Oleo Saccharum

  • 2 1⁄2 cups plantains, peeled
  • 1 cup ripe banana, peeled and sliced
  • 1 1⁄4 cups superfine sugar
  • Oil, to deep fry

Cut plantains lengthwise. Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat until the oil is hot and shimmering. Fry the plantains until golden and soft (not crispy). Remove from the pan and chop into slices. Set a slice aside to garnish the drink.

In a bowl, combine sliced plantain and sliced banana. Add sugar and mix gently to avoid mashing the fruit. Cover and let sit at room temperature for eight hours, or overnight.

Strain the syrup using a sieve; do not press. The syrup can be kept, refrigerated, for up to two weeks.

In a pinch, substitute a half-ounce of banana liqueur, such as Tempus Fugit Crème de Banane, though it won’t match the complexity of the plantain-banana syrup.

title: Beach-ify Your Liquor Cabinet with These Tropical Gins
summary:
Need to shake up your gin & tonic? These tropical bottles showcase vibrant flavors like pineapple, coconut, citrus, and hibiscus.

The post Beach-ify Your Liquor Cabinet with These Tropical Gins appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Pineapple. Citrus. Coconut. Hibiscus. These are just some of the flavorings used to make tropical gins, a growing category that evokes sun and sand.

Some are made near the beach in warm-weather locales, where refreshing gin drinks hit the spot, while others are clearly inspired by the tropics.

“Gin’s history sails through the tropics, literally and metaphorically,” explains Blake Walker, co-owner of Chin Up Bar, a new gin-focused bar in New York’s Lower East Side that stocks about 200 gins from around the world. “The history of gin parallels the history of Dutch and British colonialism and the expansion of the spice trade,” which brought the pioneers of gin and genever to far-flung locales.

While some of the key botanicals found in gin—juniper, coriander, angelica—can grow in Europe, most do not. “They’re not getting all that citrus from tropical London,” Walker quips. “That’s coming from other places. You’re getting cinnamon or cassia from India; you’re getting cardamom and other botanical ingredients from exploitative conquests around the world.

Some bar pros say tropical gins are a natural expansion of existing gin styles. “It’s a sub-category of New Western-style gins,” says Brandon Ristaino, co-founder and beverage director of the tiki bar Test Pilot in Santa Barbara, California. Compared to juniper-heavy London Dry-style gins, New Western gins play down the piney juniper component, leaving room for other flavors to shine, from mango and grapefruit to the sassy spice of nutmeg or pink peppercorn.

Ristaino also points to historical precedent, notably the short-lived prevalence of pineapple gin in England in the mid-1800s, as documented by historian and Sipsmith master distiller Jared Brown. These pineapple gins later made their way stateside but were eventually edged out by pineapple-flavored rum.

Today’s tropical gins are often made in tandem with local botanicals. Look to baobab fruit in South Africa or fragrant grains of paradise cultivated in Hawaii.

Appealing as they may be, figuring out how to mix tropical gins can be tricky business.

“There aren’t a lot of original tropical recipes based on gin,” acknowledges Kevin Beary, beverage director at Chicago tiki haven Three Dots and a Dash. Beyond the classic Saturn or the Humuhumunukunukuāpua‘a (Gesundheit! It’s an easy-sipping pineapple-and-almond sour named for Hawaii’s state fish), pros suggest mixing these spirits into gin & tonics, or daiquiri-style shaken drinks. Others are finding inspiration for building new cocktails.

After all, these bottlings “create new flavor profiles for gin that we haven’t necessarily had before,” says Walker. And while they work in drinks we already know and love, “they can serve as a jumping-off point to expand that canon.”

5 Tropical Gins to Try:

While we deliberately excluded obscure and extremely limited-edition bottlings, completists might like to know about Porter’s Tropical Old Tom Gin and That Boutique-y Gin Company’s Spit-Roasted Pineapple Gin. Bar pros love them, but good luck securing a bottle; Porter’s is the most accessible of these two, available online. That said, the following bottlings are fairly easy to procure and come well-recommended.

Bayab Palm & Pineapple Gin

This South African gin centers on the tangy fruit of the iconic baobab tree, along with palm sap sweetness and pineapple. Walker describes the effect as evoking cookie dough and strawberry-banana, with a distinctly creamy feel. We also liked the African Classic Dry Bayab Gin, with its kumquat and cardamom tones.

$37 ReserveBar

Bimini Coconut Gin

Made in Maine and fat-washed with coconut oil, this bottle has become a bartender favorite. It’s dry and crisp, with a mild coconut aroma and flavor, finishing with a hint of astringent grapefruit peel. “It’s a no-brainer for a Negroni or some sort of crushed-ice drink,” Walker says.

$37 Bitter & Bottles Spirit Shop

Condesa Gin Clásica

This enjoyable, woman owned brand from Mexico City hits all the right notes, including orange, lime peel, and sage. “It’s not a 100% tropical gin, but it’s close,” says Test Pilot’s Ristaino—and it plays well in tropical drinks.

$37 Total Wine & More

Fid Street Gin

A Hawaiian-made, London Dry-style gin, this bottling nods to the arrival of English ships in Honolulu harbor. Each sip unfurls bold citrus, fennel, and a forest-fresh note. Botanicals include Maui Gold pineapple, lavender grown on the island, and cedar leaf tips.

$32 Astor Wines and Spirits

Jin Jiji India Dry Gin

Versatile and mixable, its botanicals include wild Himalayan juniper foraged in the mountains, cashew nut, chamomile, and black tea. The result is tannic and bracing, ideal for a martini. Walker is also a fan of Jiji’s Darjeeling expression, which amps up the black-tea effect.

$23 Total Wine & More

title: Dreamy Cabernets Are Hiding in the Clouds of Moon Mountain
summary:
Powerful and energetic Cabernet Sauvignon reigns supreme in this high-altitude region of Sonoma.

The post Dreamy Cabernets Are Hiding in the Clouds of Moon Mountain appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
High in the hills above Sonoma Valley, where the peaks of the Mayacamas range form the boundary between Napa and Sonoma counties, grow some of California’s best Cabernets. In the Moon Mountain AVA, iron-rich soils, old vines, and upwards of 2,200 feet of elevation combine to create a framework for age-worthy wines that possess a true sense of place. Up here, old-school Sonoma winemaking and energetic new projects are sharing the hillsides and a vision for the future of California Cabernet.

Although the Moon Mountain AVA has been recognized only since 2013, the region’s oldest vines date back to the 1850s, when early farmers like James Hamilton (Montecillo Vineyard) and Thomas Nau (Nuns Canyon Ranch) cleared trees from elevated slopes to plant a range of popular varieties. Subsequent replantings have sharpened the focus, and now Cabernet reigns supreme. This isn’t surprising, given that the opposite slope of this hillside is the Mount Veeder AVA in Napa Valley. But the wines of Moon Mountain are distinct, bearing the clear imprint of volcanic soils and a persistent acidity that seems to resonate throughout the appellation. Wines here are powerful but seldom bombastic. Energetic, but never chaotic. It’s the mark of a truly great wine region beginning to find its footing.

Rodrigo Soto, co-owner of Far Mountain Wines and a member of the Moon Mountain District Winegrowers Association, was drawn to the magic of the area before it became a recognized appellation. “There’s something energetic about being out there,” he says “[Moon Mountain] has a special connection. It feels private, feels remote, feels wild.”

Like-minded producers here are embracing and refining organic and regenerative techniques, dry-farming, and biodynamics in pursuit of ever more exceptional wines from these slopes. Gallo’s Monte Rosso Vineyard converted to organic farming over the past few years and was certified in July 2025. Brenae Royal, who began the process when she was the ranch manager, believes that it just makes sense to farm this way on Moon Mountain. “Everybody has this same idea of wanting to increase the soil health and microbial activity and use a lot of regenerative practices,” she says.

Up Nuns Canyon Road, at some of the oldest vineyards in the AVA, Montecillo Vineyard is farming organically and preparing for certification, while Hamel Family Wines’ Nuns Canyon Vineyard is biodynamic as well as dry farmed. Stone Edge Farm’s Silver Cloud Estate Vineyard is farmed organically by Phil Coturri. The list goes on, and while there’s a variety of practices on the hill, the shared philosophy is as clear as the view from the top of the ridge: Let the land speak and let the vines do the work.

The roads up Moon Mountain aren’t necessarily easy to navigate, but the wines, the people, and the views are all worth the trip. Here are a few estates to check out if you’re craving some Cabernet or just a bit of mountain air.

Classic Moon Mountain Wineries

Stone Edge Farm

Chalone Wine Group co-founder Mac McQuown purchased Silver Cloud in 2012, just before Moon Mountain became an appellation. Only about 10% of the estate is planted to vines, but Alejandro Zimman’s style in the cellar yields complex and elegant wines that will thrill Bordeaux fans, and the views aren’t too shabby either. Stone Edge Farm also operates Enclos, a two-Michelin-starred restaurant in downtown Sonoma that’s worth a splurge.

Kamen Estate Wines

Robert Kamen, the screenwriter who penned the Karate Kid movies, was an early pioneer of Moon Mountain winemaking. His excellent, classically styled Cabernets and other wines are available at his tasting room in downtown Sonoma or at the absolutely stunning vineyard. It’s worth the drive up the hill.

Stewart Cellars

Blair Guthrie recently purchased the historic Montecillo Vineyard, which makes a lovely counterpoint to Stewart Cellars’ otherwise Napa-focused portfolio. Montecillo’s historic vines produce elegant Cabernets with energetic acidity that balances their considerable tannin. You’ll need to pop over to Napa to taste with Stewart Cellars, as the tasting room is in Yountville. This lineup is a great way to contemplate the differences between the eastern and western slopes of the Mayacamas.

New and Exciting Projects

Hamel Estate

A short drive from the town of Sonoma brings you to Hamel’s architecturally striking winery, just off the valley floor. The estate was founded in 2006 but has recently been refocused and reinvigorated. The wines here are thoughtful and exciting, with a focus on biodynamic farming, native yeasts, and a dazzling array of less-common fermentation and aging vessels. It’s an unrelenting effort to understand the vineyards and to let them speak for themselves. Be sure to try their Nuns Canyon Grenache, paying close attention to its evolution in the glass.

Mountain Terraces

This venture is tremendous, and not for the faint of heart (ATV vineyard tour, anyone?). These younger-vine wines already show awesome aging potential. With hillsides currently being tamed and new vineyard blocks being developed, it’s a great opportunity to see and learn about mountain viticulture in an early phase. The Cabernet is great, but look out for the Petit Verdot, which packs a wallop. “Up here, the vines struggle—volcanic soils, steep slopes, extreme elevation,” says Mountain Terraces owner Steve Ledsen. “But that struggle is exactly what makes the wines extraordinary.”

Violet’s Paradise

Brenae Royal doesn’t have a tasting room yet, but her passion for Moon Mountain and the Monte Rosso Vineyard is crystal clear in her limited-edition vision of the parcel. This project examines a range of varieties from the legendary site. Without a doubt, Violet’s Paradise makes a powerful Cabernet and a thought-provoking old-vine Zin, but the Grenache and a Sémillon from the world’s second-oldest vines shine brightly as well.

Visiting the Area

It’s best to stay in or near downtown Sonoma, where the lovely square offers plenty of options for lodging, food, and shopping. Locals swear by Ohm Coffee Roasters for morning or afternoon caffeination, Valley for brunch and lunch, and The Girl & The Fig for dinner, when your tastings are done for the day. If you’re the sort of wine lover who can’t live on Cabernet alone, wineries like Auteur Wines, Three Sticks, Darling Wines, and many others all have tasting rooms on or just off the square that are well worth checking out for Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and other varieties. As for Cabernet purists, this is the region you didn’t know you were looking for.

title: Mexican Winemaking Is Reaching New Heights in These High-Altitude Regions
summary:
Despite the popularity of Northern Baja California, a new generation of winemakers are embracing high-altitude regions in other parts of the country.

The post Mexican Winemaking Is Reaching New Heights in These High-Altitude Regions appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The oldest winery in the Americas is Casa Madero in Mexico’s Coahuila state. It sits at 5,000 feet in elevation, at a latitude similar to the hottest parts of the Sahara Desert, on soil dotted with dinosaur bones. When Spanish colonizers arrived, they found an abundance of wild grapes, natural springs, and a semi-arid mountain valley with a continental climate that can feel Mediterranean thanks to mild winters and a long growing season.

Spain’s King Felipe didn’t expect to find a world-class grape-growing region here, but once he did, he decreed it a center of wine and brandy production, using imported Spanish varieties. It was the first place in the Americas where Vitis vinifera grapes were successful.

Northern Baja California, centered around the Valle de Guadalupe, accounts for an estimated 80% of the country’s wine. Despite its proximity to the ocean, vineyards there are high altitude by most other wine regions’ criteria, ranging from 1,000 feet to nearly 2,300 feet. Yet 16 Mexican states make wine commercially, and attention is shifting to the historic, higher-altitude regions, like Guanajuato and Querétaro states in the center of the country, and Coahuila in the north. There, a new generation with a bold vision is crafting wines of character with minimal intervention—and the rest of the world is noticing.

Coahuila

Casa Madero has been making wine since 1597, but only in recent years has the Coahuila region’s wine industry boomed. The town of Parras (short for Parras de la Fuente, or “grape vines of the wellspring”) has become a tourist hub between the business capital of Monterrey and the industrial city of Torreón.

“Our combination of altitude, continental climate, and low humidity is an ideal ecosystem for all types of grapes,” says winemaker Matías Utrero of Rivero González Winery (a.k.a. RG|MX) in Parras. They practice low-intervention winemaking and ecological viticulture that respects native flora, reuses organic matter as compost and mulch, and promotes what he calls “living soil.” Originally from Argentina and with vineyard experience throughout the Americas, he finds the region uniquely suited to natural winemaking. “The advantages are remarkable in terms of grape health, water quality, and a wide temperature range that helps retain natural acidity, complexity, and balance.”

Mile-high Parras is, in fact, considered a “low-elevation” part of the region. Two hours east, in the mountains above the city of Saltillo, a vertiginous region known as Sierra de Arteaga reaches over 7,000 feet. Walking the vineyards too quickly there can make you short of breath. Sometimes called “La Suiza Mexicana” (the Switzerland of Mexico), its frosty peaks stand in sharp contrast to the desert plains of Parras. Located in the Sierra de Arteaga, Bodegas del Viento and Bodega los Cedros are among the highest wineries in the world.

“There’s a lot of variety between Parras and Arteaga,” says Carlos Solares, a wine-industry consultant based in Monterrey. “In Arteaga, the cooler climate is spectacular for reds; phenolic maturity is reached before alcoholic ripeness, and they harvest as late as November. In Parras, reds can be jammier. Whites are starting to stand out more, with Albariño, Verdejo, Torrontés, and Chenin Blanc showing up alongside the Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc.”

Red blends are a hallmark of Mexico, since the country doesn’t have appellations that restrict what grapes can be used. Rolling harvests during the long growing season give even more flexibility to those who put in the work. The most planted red grapes in Coahuila are Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Syrah, and Tempranillo, though Cabernet Franc, Malbec, Caladoc, Pinot Noir, and Petit Verdot are becoming more prominent.

“The best Coahuila wines avoid chemical corrections and correct in the vineyard,” says Solares. “Not just blending different varieties, but by blending the same grape at different ripeness levels, they can maximize phenolic complexity while maintaining acidity and freshness.”

Guanajuato and Querétaro

Almost 400 miles south of Coahuila lies El Bajío (The Lowland), a fertile mountain valley whose 5,000-foot elevation is dwarfed by the surrounding peaks. A few hours northwest of Mexico City, it’s home to the eponymous capitals of Querétaro and Guanajuato states, both with emerging wine scenes.

Most foreign visitors to Guanajuato come to the tourist town of San Miguel de Allende, where wine seems a distant concern from its rooftop cocktail bars. Just 45 minutes north, however, is the pueblo of Dolores Hidalgo, where the Mexican War of Independence was declared in 1810. After over a century of Spain banning wine and brandy production except for religious use (because it threatened Spanish imports), the restoration of vineyards in this region was seen as both economic and metaphorical independence.

Guanajuato has become known for natural wine, thanks to two influential wineries: Garambullo, founded in 2017 by a Mexican and Bosnian couple who have mentored several winemakers in the area, and Octágono, which makes wine in buried clay vessels, a sort of Mexican kvevri. But it’s a region where innovation takes many forms.

On the road from San Miguel to Dolores Hidalgo is Tres Raíces. Winemaker Aarón Huertas spent 15 years working in his home region of Rioja before taking an exploratory trip to the winery a few years ago. He became convinced that, with proper vineyard management, Guanajuato’s soils and climate had the potential to make the best red wines in Mexico, and he took the job. In the last two years, it’s become one of the country’s most internationally awarded wineries, moving from a bodega known for poundable rosé to a premium wine producer using estate-grown grapes.

“In Spain, cultivation is impossible over 1,000 meters [3,280 feet], but at this latitude it becomes ideal,” Huertas says, noting that Rioja’s highest vineyards max out under 3,000 feet, while the vineyards of Tres Raíces sit comfortably around 6,500 feet. “We rarely get above 90°F in the hottest months, or below 40°F in the winter, and the huge diurnal swing—sometimes exceeding 36°F—creates very strong phenolics in terms of tannins, anthocyanins, color, and structure.” A slow, gradual ripening in poor sandy-loam soil yields very high-quality fruit.

Driving 90 minutes southeast, you hit the mineral-rich volcanic soils of Querétaro state, which lies along the Trans-Mexican Volcanic Belt. The population of the sprawling capital, Santiago de Querétaro, has doubled over the past 25 years, yet its cobblestoned historic center remains one of Mexico’s most beautiful and well preserved. It’s a good base for exploring the tiny state’s wineries and its seven so-called “Pueblos Mágicos” (“magic towns” officially designated for their culture and beauty).

Xaime Niembro is the founder of Vinos Barrigones, a natural winery that shares space with its distillery, working farm, and boutique hotel—María & Bernardo Hotel Boutique. Their restaurant, Bárbaro, is one of Mexico’s best and most charming winery restaurants. The outdoor tables sit among vineyards, gardens, and open-fire grills cooking locally farmed meat. Plan an entire afternoon sipping away the day here.

“These are definitely special conditions,” Niembro says with a laugh, gesturing to the nets covering the vines. “We get hail, then drought for eight months of the year, and a semi-desert climate that’s anything but predictable, but it gives us wines with zero intervention that have a ton of aromatic expression, that never get lost in a funky character, and highlight the typicity of the land.”

Vinos Barrigones’ flagship grape is Xarel-lo (playfully labeled as Charelo), with other Cava grapes, Macabeo and Parellada, also planted. In the 1980s, the Catalan Cava juggernaut Freixenet chose Querétaro as its first production site outside Spain, effectively launching the modern-day wine industry in the state. But Barrigones’ still white blend of Xarel-lo and Macabeo (with a touch of Grenache), with its explosive aromatics and crisp flavors that move from vivid citrus to tropical fruit, hardly resembles Freixenet’s neutral bubbles.

“You can’t understand viticulture in Querétaro without Freixenet; they’re the pioneers in this field, provide advice, and still help bring grapes to Mexico, which explains the great Catalan influence on what’s planted here,” says Niembro. “They are and will be the fathers of wine in Querétaro, and from here we’ve come as the rebels who just want to explore and take our wines in other directions.”

Luis Aburto of Tierra de Peña is an architect-turned-winemaker whose bush vines thrive in the shadow of his winery’s namesake, Peña de Bernal, a phallic 1,500-foot monolith recognizable throughout Mexico. He maintains a closed-loop permaculture system (captured rainwater, grazing cows, companion planting) to maximize biodiversity and eliminate inputs and waste. His compost-rich soils sit atop tepetate (a.k.a. tuff, or compacted volcanic debris), from which he makes wines that range from Syrah rosé to a Merlot-Tempranillo-Cab blend to a Muscat pét nat. Freshness and minerality are common among the region’s wines, but as with all Mexican wine, its diversity is its strength.

“There’s no signature flavor to our wines because there are no signature grapes, no tradition to uphold, no winemaker grandparents to frustrate,” says Aburto. “When you’re dogmatic, you don’t question, so you don’t learn. As long as we work in a way that benefits the land and the neighbors, it’s ours to discover. Every year is a new revelation.”

The Best Restaurants for Local Wine

Coahuila (& Nearby)

Casa Cenizo (Parras)

Bombilla (Parras)

Vernáculo (Monterrey)

Querétaro & Guanajuato

title: Behind the Badge: The Pros and Cons of UNESCO Recognition
summary:
UNESCO inscription is seen as a crucial step in safeguarding a tradition’s future, but there's a right way and a wrong way to go about it.

The post Behind the Badge: The Pros and Cons of UNESCO Recognition appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
In the majestic setting of New Delhi’s Red Fort last December, the Intergovernmental Committee of the United Nations Educational, Scientific, and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO) announced its latest additions to the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity list. Alongside practices as diverse as Iceland’s swimming pool culture, the traditional saree-weaving art of Tangail in Bangladesh, and Italian cuisine was Commandaria, a historic sweet wine made on the island of Cyprus.

Commandaria isn’t the first vinous tradition to receive UNESCO protection. Georgia’s 8,000-year-old qvevri production method was inscribed in 2013, and a high number of wine regions—from the hills of Conegliano Valdobbiadene to Champagne—were also recognized in the separate World Heritage list (which focuses specifically on landscapes).

These acknowledgments are significant achievements for the communities involved and a source of immense national pride for their countries and regions. But beyond the prestige, a UNESCO inscription is also widely seen as a crucial step in safeguarding a tradition’s future. It brings added credibility and recognition, which can help command higher prices and stimulate investment in the sector, support economic growth, create jobs, and promote overall prosperity.

Existing examples of UNESCO-recognized heritage show how such listings can generate substantial economic opportunities for the wine industries involved. Producers of Prosecco Superiore, for example, saw a notable surge in wine tourism after the hills of Conegliano Valdobbiadene were inscribed as a World Heritage Site in 2019. 

In 2024 alone, visitor arrivals jumped 26.2% year-on-year, with overnight stays up 18.7%. 

“Tourism has increased significantly, and also in terms of quality. Naturally, with such exponential growth, mass tourism has arrived, which may be less desirable, but over time it is naturally becoming more selective,” says Luigi Stramare Bortolomiol, enologist at Spumanti Gemin. “Local services are expanding, both in gastronomy and within the wineries themselves, with cellar and vineyard visits, guided tastings, and regular weekend openings.”

Brewing Success

The organization’s formal recognition of qvevri winemaking has also been widely credited as a key driver of the international success of Georgian wine in recent years. 

According to Georgia’s national wine agency, exports of the country’s wine have grown rapidly since the inscription in 2013. The year before, 17.5 million bottles were exported; by 2013, this had nearly doubled to 33.5 million, reaching today around 100 million liters. The Qvevri narrative has also helped increase value: export revenue rose from $128 million in 2013 to $276 million today, with a growing share going to the US market.

“The main benefit our country gains from UNESCO is the promotion of Georgian cultural heritage and Georgian wine on the international stage,” says Tamta Kvelaidze, head of marketing and public relations at the National Wine Agency of Georgia. “In recent years, there has been growing international interest in qvevri, accompanied by an increase in the export of qvevri vessels… This recognition has been positively leveraged by the agency and by producers alike as a promotional tool.”

Fourth-generation qvevri master Zaza Kbilashvili runs one of the few private qvevri workshops that survived the seven decades of Soviet occupation. His family business played a crucial role in the UNESCO bid, with Kbilashvili and his father even appearing as protagonists of the short documentary featured as part of the entry. “After UNESCO inscription in 2013, qvevris began gaining proper worldwide recognition,” he says. “Business is now thriving, and given that it takes months to craft each individual qvevri, my waiting list grows longer by the day.”

The UNESCO badge can be so powerful to even impact neighboring regions or countries. 

The 2013 acknowledgement of qvevri winemaking, with its 8,000-year narrative, for instance, came at a time when Armenia was still recovering from Soviet-era disruptions to its wine industry. It shaped international perceptions of the Caucasus’ wine heritage by positioning Georgia as its preeminent vinous culture, leaving Armenia to define its wine identity largely in response to that narrative.

“Ancient wine culture in the South Caucasus is so deeply shared between the two countries, and this recognition has highlighted Georgia’s winemaking history on the international stage,” says a senior industry official, speaking on condition of anonymity. “But the framing of that history as exclusively Georgian can sometimes complicate matters for Armenia, as it suggests that any competing narrative must be positioned as a counterpoint to this dominant story… Had Georgia not received UNESCO recognition… there would likely be more room for a broader conversation about the interconnections of wine traditions in the Caucasus.”

Getting It Right from the Start

The Armenia–Georgia case demonstrates that UNESCO inclusion can sometimes have unintended consequences for neighboring regions. Yet, listing alone does not guarantee economic success for the sector concerned either, nor does it ensure the preservation of the culture itself.

Historian Yves Segers of Catholic research university KU Leuven has been involved in several UNESCO projects, including the successful Belgian beer culture bid. He argues that a real, positive outcome depends on the ongoing dedication, collaboration, and shared vision of industry members, a process that must begin from the very outset. 

“The very application process enlarges and strengthens the community,” he says. “It brings stakeholders together and encourages people to find solutions. It raises awareness of the value of [the listed] culture and gives governments and authorities the motivation to invest in keeping it alive.”

If partnership is the key to success, the latest high-profile wine-related project may well be on the right track.

The project brings together the collaboration of a coalition of consortia—Marsala, Jerez, Samos, and France’s Confédération des Vins Doux Naturels—with the goal of revitalizing the struggling fortified wine category.

The coalition’s work is already fostering inter-regional dialogue, with recent meetings even generating concrete ideas for potential joint initiatives. 

“For the first time, different wine regions with strong identities and independent histories have chosen to work together based on a shared vision,” says Benedetto Renda, president of the Consorzio Tutela Marsala. “The nomination marks the beginning of a new form of interregional and international cooperation. Each territory rightly maintains its own identity, but the foundations are being laid for a common language and for coordinated cultural and promotional projects.”

The consortia participating in the project are currently compiling their individual dossiers, which are expected to be combined and submitted as early as this coming March. Lovers of flor yeast, sun-dried grapes, and solera aging can only hope that UNESCO will soon prove a boon for yet another extraordinary wine heritage.

This can only succeed if the process itself becomes an opportunity for critical reflection and growth, carried out as a coordinated, harmonious effort among multiple voices, with the ultimate goal of preserving and sharing humanity’s collective culture and history.

title: Through Thick and Thin, Dan Duckhorn Believed in Napa Valley Merlot
summary:
As acres upon acres of Cabernet spread across the Napa Valley, Dan Duckhorn set his sights on Merlot—and changed the California wine world forever.

The post Through Thick and Thin, Dan Duckhorn Believed in Napa Valley Merlot  appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
With the recent death of winemaker Dan Duckhorn, the Napa Valley wine community loses a visionary leader who championed Merlot and changed the California wine landscape forever. 

Duckhorn, who died on Feb. 25 at the age of 87, founded Duckhorn Vineyards in 1976 with then-wife Margaret at a time when Napa Valley became ground zero for the explosion of Cabernet Sauvignon. He must have looked at that boom with a curious contrarian’s eye and simply thought, “Why not Merlot?” 

Indeed, as acres upon acres of Cabernet began to spread across the Napa Valley, Duckhorn honed a singular focus on the undersung grape, which was mostly used for blending at the time in California. 

“Dan championed Merlot as a standalone variety and elevated it to luxury status,” says Duckhorn winemaker Renée Ary. “At a time when Cabernet Sauvignon dominated the conversation, he saw that Merlot could have the structure, complexity, and ageability equal to any variety.” 

Over time, the vineyard that he chose for those earliest efforts, named Three Palms, would become synonymous both with his last name and with elite Napa Valley Merlot. The vineyard is certainly capable of producing Cabernet, and Duckhorn does, but the soul of this place is unequivocally Merlot, and for good reason. From the very beginning, Duckhorn’s Three Palms has possessed the unique combination of structure, nuance, and balance that make a wine world-class. 

A recent retrospective of Duckhorn’s wines, held shortly before his passing, celebrated the 50th anniversary of the winery, showcasing an impressive vertical tasting of Three Palms Merlots.

Vintages from the 1980s showed both elegance and staying power. Examples from the 2000s were still just coming into their own. Without a doubt, the finest wine at the table was the 1999 Three Palms made by Duckhorn’s first winemaker, Tom Rinaldi. This vintage was energetic, focused, and exceedingly complex, drinking nearly perfectly and proving beyond a shadow of a doubt that Duckhorn’s instinct to focus on Merlot was spot on. Simply put, these wines are exceptional and can age alongside the very best in the world. 

It hasn’t always been easy to be the standard bearer for Merlot in California. In the mid-2000s, as the movie Sideways raised the profile of Pinot Noir all up and down the California coast, one throwaway line nearly eradicated the reputation of one of the world’s great wine grapes. The line, “I am NOT drinking any fucking Merlot!”, spat venomously by actor Paul Giamatti, had a legitimate (and exceedingly negative) effect on Merlot sales all across America and beyond. But Duckhorn hardly blinked. 

During that brutal mid-2000s stretch, as Merlot grapes from other producers piled up and sales fell, a string of exceptional vintages cemented Duckhorn as California’s top producer of the variety. During those years, that particular Merlot was ubiquitous, and customers asked for it by name. 

Ary, who has spent over twenty years helping craft these wines, recalls Duckhorn’s unwavering passion for the region he went all-in on. “Dan believed the Napa Valley could stand shoulder-to-shoulder with the great wine regions of the world, and he was willing to stake everything on that belief,” she says. “He wasn’t interested in copying Bordeaux or chasing trends. He defined Napa on its own terms.”

It’s hard to imagine a modern wine world without pioneers like Duckhorn, who placed their trust in the land and the vines, leaving behind a piece of their cherished valley for us all to taste.

title: A Cheat Sheet to the Types of Glass Used in Stemware
summary:
If you’ve been considering upping your glassware game but don’t know where to start, learn these main glass types first.

The post A Cheat Sheet to the Types of Glass Used in Stemware appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Far from being a mere delivery system for your Merlot or Manhattan, a well-made glass enhances the drinking experience by looking (and feeling) aesthetically pleasing. In addition to coming in different shapes and styles, stemware is crafted from all sorts of materials—each with its own benefits and drawbacks. 

If you’ve been considering upping your glassware game but don’t know where to start, learn these glass types first. Below, sommeliers, bartenders, and other drinks professionals offer their takes on which to pick up—and when to use them.

The Glass Types in Wine Stemware, Explained

All glass is not created equally. Learn these common terms before you make your next purchase.

Machine-Made

The least expensive stemware is machine-made, where molten glass is poured or pressed into molds. While the resulting glassware is uniform and durable, it also tends to be thicker, with an unsightly seam and a telltale indentation on the bottom of the base. 

“While machine-made glassware may not create the ideal aesthetics for an upscale wine tasting, it ensures you get a consistent sip, especially when comparing wines,” says Jim Pennella, owner of Farang Wine and Harmony School Vineyard, in Napa.

Machine-Blown

A step up is machine-blown, which uses an automation process to mimic glassblowing techniques, rendering a lighter, more refined glass. Riedel’s glassware is machine blown, as are Wine Enthusiast’s Pirouette and Vienna glasses.

Austin Bridges, wine director of Nostrana in Portland, Oregon, loves the shape of the Gabriel-Glas StandArt glass, which is machine-blown from one piece of crystal without joints, seams, or glue. 

“It showcases any wine beautifully from Champagne to an aged Barolo, plus it’s incredibly durable.” Moreover, its seamless design makes it more prone to chipping rather than fully breaking, making it unsightly but still usable.

Mouth-Blown and Handblown

Mouth-blown or handblown stemware (the two terms are used interchangeably) lie on the more artisanal side of the glassware spectrum. These are made by blowing air into molten glass and shaping it by hand. Zalto’s glassware, which is considered to be top of the line, is mouth-blown into molds.

“This is a handmade process,” says Anna-Christina Cabrales, Tasting Director at Wine Enthusiast. Mouth-blown glasses can take almost any shape, and no two pieces are the same—each is unique.” 

Glassware that’s handmade may be adorned with intricate designs. That means it comes with a hefty price tag—a consideration if you’re running a busy wine bar, frequently entertaining at home, or are just accident-prone. 

Crystal

Stemware choices don’t stop at the production method. Though people often use the term crystal as a synonym for glass, the phrase actually refers to a specific kind of glass that contains added minerals, often lead oxide. 

This lends a sparkly appearance that enhances the clarity, strength, and durability of the glass, and enables drinkers to see the wine within more easily. Lead-free alternatives like barium, zinc, or potassium retain the same attributes but are even more durable and can withstand being cleaned in the dishwasher. 

Lead-free crystal is the gold standard for high-end wine glasses because of how thin it can be made. “Crystal’s thin walls and ultra-thin rims allow wine to flow freely over your palate to assess the flavor of the wine fully,” Pennella says. 

Borosilicate 

Borosilicate glasses, which contain silica and boron trioxide, are another dishwasher-safe option. This is a thin, durable glass—often used in lab equipment and in the original Pyrex products—that will not crack as easily when exposed to the heat of a drying cycle.

Break-Resistant

If glassware is labeled as break-resistant or shatter-resistant, it means it’s produced from a material that makes it less prone to breakage. 

Unbreakable Glasses

While a crystal glass might be instantly destroyed if it’s knocked over on the counter, a shatter-resistant one may withstand that kind of fumble.

Finally, unbreakable “glasses” aren’t actually made of glass at all, but a copolyester plastic. Those crafted from Eastman Tritan copolyester (what Wine Enthusiast uses for its Brilliance line) are strong, easily malleable, and extremely durable, as well as FDA-approved and BPA-free.

Choosing the Right Glass Type

No matter the material, beverage pros cite one sought-after characteristic that makes for a “perfect” glass: how it performs when you drink from it.

“The main thing that affects the drinking experience is the thickness of the glassware,” says Jeff Cleveland, general manager of Birch, in Milwaukee. “The thinner the lip, the nicer it feels.” 

This is where production methods come into play. “The thinner glass walls and differing shapes of mouth-blown glassware may affect how we perceive aromas,” Pennella says. 

So, out of the dizzying array of stemware on the market, how do you know which is right for you? 

Pennella believes it’s situational. He suggests machine-made glassware that meets ISO standards for comparative tastings (these vessels are made from crystal, hold around 7 ounces of liquid, are six inches tall and around 2.5 inches wide) and mouth-blown crystal (lead or lead-free) for a more elevated experience. If you’re sipping wine by the pool, beach, or windy location, go for break-resistant or borosilicate, which, he says, has the added benefit of maintaining temperature.

If you’re a clumsy butterfingers but don’t want a clunky, thick stem, Bridges touts a seamless, thin-rimmed crystal machine-blown glass. “Glasses with seams tend to snap right at the seam during polishing or if they’re set down a bit too firmly, he says.”

When sourcing glasses for his contemporary Midwestern restaurant, Cleveland considers durability as well as aesthetics, because a delicate, thin-rimmed glass is no good if its time on the shelf is fleeting thanks to rough handling or easy breakage. For these reasons, he’s partial to Riedel’s Restaurant series, which is elegant without being too fragile.

Beau du Bois, vice president of bar and spirits at Roma Norte in San Diego, thinks about what he calls “the path of the glass,” meaning where the vessel is stored during service, where it goes once the guest has finished sipping, and how it’s cleaned—a philosophy that can be adopted by the home bartender. “It’s best to consider all these factors before getting your heart set on that new Zalto tulip, no matter how sexy it is.”

Benjamin, a steakhouse in New York, recently switched to the break-resistant Vienna glass line from Wine Enthusiast for its wine and water service. This move has helped them balance performance with aesthetics. “Our day to day breakage is a third less than with our previous line of stemware, plus the bigger sized bowls look elegant and stunning on the table,” says Albert Belegu, the restaurant’s general manager. 

Oenophiles planning on conducting serious wine tastings should consider glasses that balance durability with thinness. Sometimes, one glass isn’t enough. Cabrales advises investing in at least one pair of glasses with a good proportion of crystal, plus something more durable. 

“I want to have a sophisticated delivery into the mouth and overall feeling in the hand,” she says. “At home, I have two sets of stemware: those that help me get friends who are just starting into wine, and high-end ones that impress my nerdy wine friends. It’s all about style.”

title: Is It Time to Refresh Your Stems? 3 Signs Your Glassware Needs an Upgrade
summary:
How do you know if your glassware is up for the job—and when it’s time to upgrade? These are three signs to look for.

The post Is It Time to Refresh Your Stems? 3 Signs Your Glassware Needs an Upgrade appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There’s nothing worse than going to a restaurant, excited to order wine with dinner, and receiving it in a sub-par glass. (There’s a reason stubby stems are the meme that keeps on giving.) 

But everyone’s definition of “nice glasses” is different. I, for one, can’t stand bubbly in one of those clunky diner flutes intended for bottomless mimosas. I’m not anti-flute by any means—but I am anti-clunk. In fact, I even switched wedding caterers two weeks before my wedding because the stems they presented as the “upgraded” option were so chunky they made 1980s ashtrays look elegant. 

But one woman’s chonk is another woman’s lead-free crystal. The most important thing is that glassware performs as it should, and ideally looks beautiful doing so. So how do you know if your glassware is up for the job—and when it’s time to upgrade? It turns out, there are a few signs.

Sign #1: Your stems aren’t trustworthy anymore

If any part of your glass feels unstable, it’s not “character,” it’s a liability—and nothing kills the vibe faster than a trip to the ER for stitches. “First, look for wobble or instability on a flat surface,” says Dr. Lee Kojanis, co-founder of MITTS wine sponge. “A glass that rocks, leans, or feels even slightly off-balance can signal subtle warping or micro-damage to the stem or base—often the kind you don’t notice until it fails mid-polish or, worse, mid-toast.”

There’s also the rim. While everyone loves a delicate glass, damage to the rim should be an immediate sign it’s time to put that stem out to pasture. “A tiny nick may seem harmless, but it’s both a safety issue and a quality issue,” says Kojanis. “It can worsen with the next rinse or polish, and it disrupts the smooth, clean line that makes a glass feel refined on the lip.” 

Sign #2: They look clean, but they’re not performing

If you’ve ever poured a good wine into a clean glass expecting wow but instead getting womp womp, you’re already familiar with this sign. Some glasses look obviously cloudy. For others, the culprit is invisible until you hold the bowl up to the light and notice a film, etching, or fine scratching that builds up over years of normal use.

“Detergent film and calcium deposits leave a subtle haze, muting the wine’s aromas and flavors,” says Kurt Zalto, sixth-generation glassmaker and co-founder of glassware line Josephinenhütte. “Micro-scratches from routine cleaning can scatter light and dull the wine’s brilliance. If your glass isn’t crystal clear, it’s not performing at its best.”

And the problem isn’t just aesthetic. “That microscopic wear can also create a surface that holds onto residue and interferes with the crisp visual and aromatic experience you’re chasing,” adds Kojanis.

Given that we’re all guilty of shoving glassware into storage at some point, make sure to do the sniff test. Whether that storage looks like a top shelf in your kitchen, a rack in your dining room, or a crate somewhere with holiday decorations and your high school yearbooks, weird smells can get trapped in the bowl of your glass. 

“Cabinet odor is real and can alter how your beverage tastes,” says lifestyle content creator Lindsay Stewart. “If you store your stemware in boxes in a pantry, or in the back of a cabinet, check your glass for musty, dusty, or woody funk.”

Sign #3: Your glassware doesn’t match how you drink now

This is the sneakiest sign because it’s not about what you own—it’s about what you reach for. If your “good glasses” only come out once a decade, your glassware is failing you.

“People tend to be afraid to use their good stems as they’re afraid the glasses are too nice, and should only be used for that special bottle that they never open anyway as they never find a good occasion to drink it,” says sommelier Liora Levi, co-host of PBS show Wine First and a Riedel Ambassador.

Levi also calls out the classics that tend to linger in cabinets long past their prime. “Champagne coupes and flutes, old-fashioned white wine glasses, and thick and bulky wine glasses.” And then there’s the vacation-glass trap: “Sometimes people want to recreate the vacation feeling by using regular water or milk glasses, which is a terrible idea when you’ve spent a good amount on a nice bottle.” In other words, save that memory of sipping Chianti from a tumbler at a quaint Italian trattoria where it belongs—in the memory bank. 

If budget is the barrier, you can still make smart moves without pretending everyone needs a museum-quality collection. “Prioritize buying a ‘universal’ white and ‘universal’ red wine glass,” Levi advises. And Stewart’s budget approach will help keep the prices under control: “If you need stemware for a crowd on a budget, secondhand shopping is my favorite,” she says. “You can inexpensively curate your collection over time, including incorporating different heights, shapes, or styles. The mismatched look feels intentional when you have even numbers of each style.”

The goal isn’t perfection. Rather, it’s about having stems you trust, stems that make wine taste like itself, and stems you’ll actually use—yes, even on a Tuesday—without haze, wobble, or emergency stitches.

title: What, Exactly, Is ‘Break-Resistant’ Glassware?
summary:
While not shatterproof, break-resistant glassware can help protect your stemware from butterfingered friends.

The post What, Exactly, Is ‘Break-Resistant’ Glassware? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Do wine glasses tend to slip out of your grasp as you dry them? Does boisterous hand gesturing during wine time with friends cause you to inadvertently topple over your stemware? If so, you might want to consider investing in some break-resistant glassware.

But before you get too excited at the prospect of being able to drop vessels on the table or floor sans cracking or shattering, it’s helpful to dive into what this label on a glass does—and doesn’t—mean, and what you can expect if your butterfingers strike again.

When a glass is labeled “break-resistant,” it means it’s “engineered to withstand more impact and stress than traditional glass or crystal,” says Marshall Tilden III, President of Wine Express and Wine Education at Wine Enthusiast.

Manufacturers incorporate materials like titanium, reinforced borosilicate, or magnesium into regular glass in order to make the material more durable.

They also use processes like thermal tempering, in which glass is heated to a high temperature and then rapidly cooled, and chemical tempering, which uses a molten potassium nitrate bath to harden the glass, to strengthen its structure at a molecular level.

These techniques often result in glasses that have a bit of “give,” bolstering them with shock absorption that allows them to withstand a certain amount of impact without damage.

What to Expect from Break-Resistant Glassware

Let’s be clear: break-resistant does not mean break-proof.

While the addition of minerals and other steps during the manufacturing process can increase the resilience, you’ll still want to be sure to handle it carefully—and manage expectations.

“Extreme force, like being slammed against a hard surface, drastic temperature changes (such as pouring boiling water into a frozen glass), or deep scratches that compromise the integrity can still cause them to shatter,” Tilden cautions. After all, they’re still made from glass.

With that being said, the added hardening process does make a difference. Albert Belegu, general manager of Benjamin Restaurant Group in New York, is partial to the Vienna line of break-resistant stemware, which is machine-blown in Europe from lead-free crystal.

He touts them for their elegance, height, and large bowl, providing wine with ample room to breathe—and that the stem and base are connected as one unit. (When most glasses break, it’s at the point where the stem meets the base.) Since the glass is lead-free, it also offers remarkable clarity.

In Belegu’s experience, if one of these glasses does break, it’s more apt to come apart in larger chunks that are safer to handle. He likens it to the crack you might get from an object hitting a car’s windshield versus the complete shatter that might occur if the same object hits one of the windows (which are made from tempered glass).

Jim Pennella, owner of Farang Wine and Harmony School Vineyard in Napa, believes break-resistant glassware can be the perfect choice in certain situations.

“Break-resistant glass still allows you to smell, view, and taste your wine and might be the best option for large or family-friendly gatherings,” Pennella says.

The same logic goes for high-volume restaurants that endure frequent breakage with standard wine glasses—break-resistant stemware might be worth stocking up on.

What About ‘Shatterproof’ Glass?

If break-resistant glass is less prone to chips, cracks, and breaks, shatterproof options go a step further. “These are typically made from ultra-durable, BPA-free plastics or silicone-based materials like Tritan, meaning they won’t crack or splinter at all, even upon impact,” Tilden explains.

They’re not technically “glasses,” though depending on the style, they may have a similar appearance—like these Tritan outdoor wine glasses.

Shatterproof wine glasses can often come in bright colors and fun designs, too. They’re ideal options for barbecues, pool parties, boating, camping, outdoor concerts, or large gatherings where children (who are notorious for dropping things) will be present. 

In a nutshell, these types of stemware can still give you an elegant experience, but with less of a chance that it (and your night) will end up in pieces.

As Tilden puts it, “break-resistant glass can still maintain clarity and a lightweight feel while significantly reducing the risk of breakage.”
